metadata.description: Vessel discharge rates and costs for imported cement and clinker at US Gulf and Atlantic port terminals. Rates represent typical sustained throughput under normal operating conditions.
sources: International Association of Ports and Harbors (IAPH)

discharge_methods.equipment_description: Fixed or mobile vacuum-type unloading system that conveys cement/clinker powder through enclosed pipelines from vessel hold to shore silo. Dust-free enclosed system.

power_requirements.power_source: Shore power (440V/60Hz) or self-contained diesel generator

advantages: Highest throughput rate for finished cement
Fully enclosed system minimizes dust emissions
Meets EPA and state air quality requirements
Direct pipeline to silo eliminates intermediate handling
disadvantages: High capital cost ($3-5 million per unit)
discharge_methods.weather_sensitivity: Low - enclosed system operates in most weather. High winds (>40 mph) may halt hatch operations.
discharge_methods.notes: Preferred method for finished cement. Most Lower Mississippi terminals have at least one pneumatic unloader. Rate assumes single unloader; dual-unloader terminals can achieve 700-800 TPH.

discharge_methods.equipment: Mobile harbor crane with clamshell grab bucket discharging to receiving hopper
discharge_methods.equipment_description: Shore-based or floating crane equipped with 15-25 cubic meter clamshell grab. Grabs cement/clinker from vessel hold and deposits into a receiving hopper connected to enclosed conveyor system leading to storage.
advantages: Versatile - handles both fine cement and coarse clinker
Lower capital cost than pneumatic unloader
Mobile cranes can be repositioned between berths
Can work multiple hatches simultaneously with multiple cranes
disadvantages: Higher dust generation than pneumatic (requires suppression)
Cycle time affected by crane swing radius and hold depth
Grab wear increases maintenance costs with abrasive clinker
Spillage at hopper transfer point requires cleanup
discharge_methods.weather_sensitivity: Moderate - wind gusts >30 mph affect grab accuracy. Rain causes cement hardening issues.
discharge_methods.notes: Preferred method for clinker discharge. Many terminals use combination of grab crane (for clinker) and pneumatic (for cement). Rate assumes single crane; dual-crane operations achieve 500-600 TPH.

discharge_methods.equipment: Vessel's own derricks/cranes with grab bucket
discharge_methods.equipment_description: Uses the vessel's installed cargo handling gear (derricks or deck cranes, typically 25-30 ton SWL) with clamshell grab to discharge cargo to shore hopper or directly to trucks/barges.
advantages: Can discharge at terminals without unloading equipment
Useful at secondary or undeveloped berths
disadvantages: Slowest discharge rate of the three methods
Higher labor cost per ton due to longer operation time
Vessel gear typically less powerful than shore cranes
Vessel bears gear maintenance cost (reflected in freight rate)
Not available on gearless vessels (most Panamax)
discharge_methods.weather_sensitivity: High - exposed operations affected by wind, rain, and visibility. Operations typically cease at wind >25 mph.
discharge_methods.notes: Used primarily at secondary terminals lacking shore equipment, or as supplemental discharge alongside shore equipment working other hatches. Common for small parcels or opportunistic discharge at multi-use berths. Rate assumes single crane; geared vessels with twin cranes can achieve 300-350 TPH.

combined_operations.description: Many terminals deploy multiple methods simultaneously on different hatches to maximize discharge rate.

examples.typical_use: Large Supramax/Panamax with mixed cement and clinker parcels
Geared Handysize at terminal with pneumatic unloader

demurrage_context.description: Vessel discharge rates directly impact demurrage costs. Faster discharge = lower demurrage exposure.
demurrage_context.laytime_calculation_notes: Standard laytime for cement: 5,000-8,000 tons per weather working day (WWWD) depending on terminal and contract terms.
